title: CSS Fundamentals
description: Styling web pages with CSS — syntax, properties, layout, and best practices.

sections.title: Introduction to CSS
sections.lesson:
Introduction

What is CSS?

CSS is the language we use to style a Web page.

  • CSS stands for Cascading Style Sheets
  • CSS describes how HTML elements are to be displayed on screen, paper, or in other media
  • CSS saves a lot of work. It can control the layout of multiple web pages all at once
  • External stylesheets are stored in CSS files

Why Use CSS?

CSS is used to define styles for your web pages, including the design, layout and variations in display for different devices and screen sizes.

CSS Example



body
{
background-color: lightblue;
}
h1
{
color: white;
text-align: center;
}
p
{
font-family: verdana;
font-size: 20px;
}

CSS Saves a Lot of Work!

The CSS definitions are normally saved in an external .css file.

sections.title: How to Add CSS
sections.lesson:
How To Add CSS

How to Add CSS

When a browser reads a style sheet, it will format the HTML document according to the information in the style sheet.

There are three ways of inserting a style sheet:

  • External CSS
  • Internal CSS
  • Inline CSS

External CSS

With an external style sheet, you can change the look of an entire website by changing just one file!

Each HTML page must include a reference to the external style sheet file inside the element, inside the head section.

Example


<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="mystyle.css">
</head>
<body>
<h1>This is a heading</h1>
<p>This is a paragraph.</p>
</body>
</html>

External styles are defined within the element, inside the

section of an HTML page: 

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="mystyle.css">
</head>
<body>

<h1>This is a heading</h1>
<p>This is a paragraph.</p>
</body>

</html>

An external style sheet can be written in any text editor, and must be saved with a .css extension.

The external .css file should not contain any HTML tags.

Here is how the "mystyle.css" file looks:

"mystyle.css"


body {
background-color: lightblue;
}
h1 {
color: navy;
margin-left: 20px;
}

Note: Do not add a space between the property value (20) and the unit (px): Incorrect (space): margin-left: 20 px; Correct (no space): margin-left: 20px;

Internal CSS

An internal style sheet may be used if one single HTML page has a unique style.

Internal styles are defined within the <style> element, inside the <head> section of an HTML page: 

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<style>
body {
  background-color: linen;
}

h1 {
  color: maroon;
  margin-left: 40px;
}
</style>
</head>
<body>

<h1>This is a heading</h1>
<p>This is a paragraph.</p>

</body>
</html>

Inline CSS

An inline style may be used to apply a unique style for a single element.

To use inline styles, add the style attribute to the relevant element. The style attribute can contain any CSS property.

Example



<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<body>
<h1 style="color:blue;text-align:center;">This
 is a heading</h1>
<p style="color:red;">This is a paragraph.</p>
</body>
</html>

Tip: An inline style loses many of the advantages of a style sheet (by mixing content with presentation). Use this method sparingly.

Multiple Style Sheets

If some properties have been defined for the same selector (element) in different style sheets, the value from the last read style sheet will be used.


h1
{
color: navy;
}

Assume that an external style sheet has the following style for the <h1> element:



h1
{
color: navy;
}




h1
{
color: orange;
}

Then, assume that an internal style sheet also has the following style for the <h1> element:



h1
{
color: orange;
}

Example



<head>
<link rel="stylesheet" type="text/css" href="mystyle.css">
<style>
h1 {
color: orange;
}
</style>
</head>

Example



<head>
<style>
h1 {
color: orange;
}
</style>
<link rel="stylesheet" type="text/css" href="mystyle.css">
</head>

However, if the internal style is defined before the link to the external style sheet, the <h1> elements will be "navy":


<head>
<style>
h1 {
color: orange;
}
</style>
<link rel="stylesheet" type="text/css" href="mystyle.css">
</head>

Cascading Order

What style will be used when there is more than one style specified for an HTML element?

All the styles in a page will "cascade" into a new "virtual" style sheet by the following rules, where number one has the highest priority:

  1. Inline style (inside an HTML element)
  2. External and internal style sheets (in the head section)
  3. Browser default

So, an inline style has the highest priority, and will override external and internal styles and browser defaults.

sections.title: CSS Syntax
sections.lesson:
Syntax

CSS Syntax

A CSS rule consists of a selector and a declaration block:

The selector points to the HTML element you want to style.

The declaration block contains one or more declarations separated by semicolons.

Each declaration includes a CSS property name and a value, separated by a colon.

Multiple CSS declarations are separated with semicolons, and declaration blocks are surrounded by curly braces.

Example

In this example all <p> elements will be center-aligned, with a red text color:


    
p
{
color: red;
text-align: center;
}
  • p is a selector in CSS (it points to the HTML element you want to style: <p>).
  • color is a property, and red is the property value
  • text-align is a property, and center is the property value

You will learn much more about CSS selectors and CSS properties in the next chapters!

sections.title: CSS Properties
sections.lesson:
1. color and background-color

The color property sets the text color, while background-color defines the background color of an element.

 
p {
  color: white;
  background-color: teal;
}

2. font-family and font-size

These properties define the typeface and the size of the text. It’s best to include backup fonts for compatibility.

 
p {
  font-family: 'Arial', sans-serif;
  font-size: 18px;
}

3. margin and padding

margin controls the space outside an element’s border, while padding controls the space inside the border.

 
div {
  margin: 20px;
  padding: 10px;
}

4. border

The border property adds outlines around elements. You can set width, style, and color.

 
div {
  border: 2px solid #04AA6D;
}

5. width and height

These define the size of elements. Use percentages for responsiveness, or pixels for fixed dimensions.

 
img {
  width: 100px;
  height: 100px;
}

6. display and position

display controls how elements are shown (block, inline, flex, etc.), and position determines their placement on the page.

 
div {
  display: flex;
  position: relative;
}

7. text-align

The text-align property specifies the horizontal alignment of text inside an element.

 
h1 {
  text-align: center;
}

8. background-image

Add an image as a background using background-image. Combine it with background-size and background-repeat for better control.

 
body {
  background-image: url('background.jpg');
  background-size: cover;
  background-repeat: no-repeat;
}

9. box-shadow and border-radius

Use these for subtle visual effects. box-shadow adds depth, and border-radius makes corners round.

 
div {
  box-shadow: 0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.2);
  border-radius: 10px;
}

10. hover (pseudo-class)

The :hover pseudo-class changes the style of an element when a user hovers over it.

 
button:hover {
  background-color: orange;
}

11. align-items and justify-content

These properties are used inside flex containers to control the alignment of items. justify-content aligns items horizontally, while align-items aligns them vertically. 

 
.container {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: center;  /* horizontal alignment */
  align-items: center;      /* vertical alignment */
  height: 200px;
  background-color: #f1f1f1;
}

These properties form the foundation of CSS styling. Mastering them helps you design modern, responsive, and attractive web pages.

sections.lesson:

sections.title: Background & Colors
sections.lesson:
CSS Colors and Backgrounds

CSS allows you to set the colors and background styles of HTML elements using properties like color, background-color, background-image, and more.

🎨 Text Color

The color property sets the color of text. You can use color names, HEX, RGB, or HSL values.

p { color: blue; }
h1 { color: #ff6600; }
h2 { color: rgb(0, 128, 0); }

Heading Example

This is a paragraph with color applied.

🌈 Background Color

The background-color property defines the background color of an element.

div { background-color: lightblue; }
p { background-color: yellow; }

This paragraph will change its background color.

🖼️ Background Image

The background-image property allows you to set an image as the background of an element.

div {
  background-image: url('https://www.w3schools.com/css/img_tree.png');
  background-repeat: no-repeat;
  background-size: cover;
}

Click the button to apply a background image.

✨ Summary

  • color – sets the text color.
  • background-color – sets the background color.
  • background-image – sets an image as background.
  • background-repeat, background-size, and background-position – control how the image is displayed.

sections.title: CSS Display Property
sections.lesson:
Display types

Select a display value to see details.
Try changing the display and observe how boxes rearrange.
A
B
C
D

Live CSS

.sample-container { display: block; }
.sample { margin: 6px; }

Quick notes

You can also select display:none to hide the whole container. Use flex and grid to build layouts.
Built for teaching — shows behaviour differences between block, inline, inline-block, flex, grid, table, and none.

sections.title: CSS Cascading
sections.lesson:
What is CSS Cascading?

When multiple CSS rules target the same HTML element, the cascade determines which style is applied.

The Cascade Order

PriorityFactorDescription
1ImportanceRules with !important override all others.
2SpecificityMore specific selectors win over general ones.
3Source OrderIf specificity is the same, the rule appearing later wins.

Specificity Calculation

Selector TypeExampleSpecificity Value
Inline styles<h1 style="color:red">1000
ID selector#title { }100
Class, attribute, pseudo-class.main, [type="text"], :hover10
Element or pseudo-elementp, div, ::before1

Example:

#main p.highlight {
  color: blue;
}

Specificity: 100 (ID) + 10 (class) + 1 (element) = 111

Source Order Example

p {
  color: red;
}
p {
  color: blue;
}

Result: Text appears blue because the last rule wins.

References

  • W3Schools: CSS How To
  • MDN Web Docs: CSS Cascade
  • MDN Web Docs: CSS Specificity
  • FreeCodeCamp: Understanding CSS Specificity and Cascade

margin, border, padding, width, display: block/inline/inline-block
Positioning & Flow

Intermediate

Align, justify, container & items, common patterns

Tools & Ecosystem

sections.title: CSS Errors & Debugging
sections.lesson:
1. Missing Semicolons

Each CSS property declaration should end with a semicolon (;). Forgetting one can cause the following properties to fail.

Incorrect:

h1 {
  color: blue
  text-align: center;
}

Correct:

h1 {
  color: blue;
  text-align: center;
}

2. Using the Wrong Property Name

CSS property names are case-sensitive and must be spelled correctly. Typos will cause them to be ignored.

Incorrect:

p {
  font-colour: red;
}

Correct:

p {
  color: red;
}

3. Missing or Mismatched Braces

Forgetting a closing brace (}) breaks your stylesheet, as CSS will not know where a rule ends.

Incorrect:

div {
  background-color: yellow;
  color: black;

Correct:

div {
  background-color: yellow;
  color: black;
}

4. Wrong Selector Syntax

Make sure to use the correct symbols for selectors. A dot (.) is used for classes, and a hash (#) is used for IDs.

Incorrect:

p%intro {
  color: green;
}

Correct:

#intro {
  color: green;
}

5. Forgetting Units

Numeric values for dimensions (like width, height, margin) need units such as px, em, or %.

Incorrect:

div {
  width: 100;
}

Correct:

div {
  width: 100px;
}

6. Incorrect Comment Syntax

CSS comments must start with /* and end with */. HTML-style comments (<!-- ... -->) will not work.

Incorrect:

/-- This is a comment --/
p { color: red; }

Correct:

/* This is a comment */
p { color: red; }

7. Misusing Shorthand Properties

When using shorthand (like margin or background), ensure the correct order and values are provided.

Incorrect:

margin: 10px 5px 2px;

Correct:

margin: 10px 5px 2px 5px;

8. Case Sensitivity Issues

CSS property names and values are case-sensitive in some cases. Stick to lowercase for consistency.

Incorrect:

BODY {
  Background-Color: Red;
}

Correct:

body {
  background-color: red;
}

9. Incorrect Use of Quotes

When specifying font names or URLs that include spaces, use quotes properly.

Incorrect:

font-family: Times New Roman;

Correct:

font-family: "Times New Roman", serif;

10. Using Inline CSS Excessively

Overusing inline CSS makes your code harder to maintain. Use external or internal stylesheets instead.

Example:

<p style="color:blue; font-size:16px;">Hello</p>

Better approach:

p {
  color: blue;
  font-size: 16px;
}

Reference: W3Schools CSS Errors

sections.title: CSS with Bootstrap
sections.lesson:
What is Bootstrap?

Bootstrap is a popular front-end framework that helps developers create responsive, mobile-first websites easily. It includes ready-made CSS, JavaScript, and components for faster web development.

How to Install Bootstrap

You can add Bootstrap to your project in two common ways:

Option 1: Using CDN (Recommended for Beginners)

Add the following links inside the <head> of your HTML document:

<link href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.3/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" rel="stylesheet">
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.3/dist/js/bootstrap.bundle.min.js"></script>

Option 2: Downloading Bootstrap

Download it from the official Bootstrap website and include the CSS and JS files manually.

Why Use Bootstrap?

  • ✅ Responsive Design — your layout adjusts to any screen size.
  • ⚡ Faster Development — includes pre-designed components.
  • 🎨 Consistent Design — ensures a uniform look and feel.
  • 🔧 Customizable — easily change colors, spacing, and typography.

Example:

<button class="btn btn-primary">Click Me</button>

Output:

Understanding Containers

Containers are the building blocks of Bootstrap layouts. They help align content and give it a proper width depending on the screen size.

Container Type Description
.container Fixed-width container that adjusts based on screen size.
.container-fluid Full-width container that spans the entire viewport.

Example:

<div class="container">
  <h3>Fixed Width Container</h3>
</div>

<div class="container-fluid bg-light">
  <h3>Full Width Container</h3>
</div>

Bootstrap Grid System Basics

The Bootstrap grid system helps you design web pages that automatically adjust for different screen sizes. It uses a flexible 12-column layout, allowing you to organize content easily.

How the Grid Works

  • The grid system is built with .container (or .container-fluid), .row, and .col classes.
  • Content must be placed inside columns, and only columns may be immediate children of rows.
  • Rows are used to create horizontal groups of columns.
  • Columns automatically resize to fill the row — or you can specify their width.
Class Screen Size Description
.col- Extra small (<576px) Always horizontal
.col-sm- Small (≥576px) Stack vertically below 576px
.col-md- Medium (≥768px) Stack below 768px
.col-lg- Large (≥992px) Stack below 992px
.col-xl- Extra large (≥1200px) Stack below 1200px

Example 1: Equal Columns

<div class="container">
  <div class="row text-center">
    <div class="col bg-primary text-white p-3">Column 1</div>
    <div class="col bg-success text-white p-3">Column 2</div>
    <div class="col bg-warning text-dark p-3">Column 3</div>
  </div>
</div>
Column 1
Column 2
Column 3

Example 2: Responsive Columns

This example shows how columns stack on smaller devices and align side-by-side on larger screens.

<div class="container">
  <div class="row text-center">
    <div class="col-sm-4 bg-info text-white p-3">col-sm-4</div>
    <div class="col-sm-8 bg-secondary text-white p-3">col-sm-8</div>
  </div>
</div>
col-sm-4
col-sm-8

Example 3: Mixed Column Sizes

<div class="container">
  <div class="row text-center">
    <div class="col-sm-3 bg-danger text-white p-3">col-sm-3</div>
    <div class="col-sm-6 bg-success text-white p-3">col-sm-6</div>
    <div class="col-sm-3 bg-warning text-dark p-3">col-sm-3</div>
  </div>
</div>
col-sm-3
col-sm-6
col-sm-3

Example 4: Nesting Columns

You can place a new .row inside an existing column to create nested layouts.

<div class="container">
  <div class="row text-center">
    <div class="col-sm-8 bg-light border p-3">
      <p>Main Column</p>
      <div class="row">
        <div class="col-6 bg-primary text-white p-2">Nested 1</div>
        <div class="col-6 bg-success text-white p-2">Nested 2</div>
      </div>
    </div>
    <div class="col-sm-4 bg-secondary text-white p-3">Side Column</div>
  </div>
</div>

Main Column

Nested 1
Nested 2
Side Column

Summary

  • Bootstrap uses a 12-column grid for flexible layouts.
  • Use .container (or .container-fluid), .row, and .col classes to structure content.
  • Columns can be responsive, automatically stacking or aligning based on screen size.
  • Nesting allows more complex layouts inside columns.

References

  • Bootstrap 5 Documentation: Grid System
  • W3Schools: Bootstrap Grid Basic
  • MDN: Responsive Design
